entry.title: Re: B1iF Message Failure: Outbound FIE001 basePath or pattern is 'null' java.io.IOException
entry.content:
Hello SGE,

To test the issue, please try starting the SAP Integration Service using a different user account. The Local System account has only local permissions and cannot access network paths. By running the service under an account with the necessary network permissions, you may resolve the access problem.

This should help and solve the issue.

entry.title: Re: ERROR: Upgrading @cap/cds and @cap/cds-dk to version 8
entry.content:
Hey @BastiMankel ,

first of all, when working with @sap/cds@8.3.1 and @sap/cds-dk@8.3.0 everything works except for the $metadata request.

After debugging through your sample code with @hose versions I saw, that the metadata error is cause by the metadata middleware (@sap/cds/libx/odata/middleware/metadata.js => line 66). This relies on having either cds.context.model or cds.model being properly filled, which is not the case.

I couldn't find out, why both of which are undefined, especially since the `service` variable shows, that the model is properly loaded.

Tweeking line 66 of the metadata.js from 

const csnService = (cds.context.model || cds.model).definitions[service.definition.name]

to

const csnService = (cds.context.model || cds.model || service.model).definitions[service.definition.name]

makes the metadata work again, but that is something, that needs to be addressed to the SAP team working on @sap/cds or SAP Cloud SDK, I think. 

When I tried comparing the differences in that metadata middleware between @sap/cds@7.9.3 and @sap/cds@8.3.1, I noticed, that middleware is not activated in version 7.9.3 but is in 8.3.1 without having done anything within your project setup. Unfortunately, I did not find any hint on that behaviour change in the CAPire docs.

Looking forward to seeing any progress on that topic since at the moment it's blocking our migration to CAPv8...

entry.title: Re: Unable to create a technical user in SAP HANA cockpit
entry.content: Possible reasons for this issue include user permissions, password policy, user limit, system resources, and errors. Ensure your user has necessary privileges, your password meets SAP HANA's policy, you haven't reached the user limit, your system has enough resources, and check for error messages in system logs. If issues persist, contact SAP Support.

entry.title: Re: How to retrieve data from a fieldymbol nested of deep...
entry.content:
The solution looks a bit different, but anyhow your "LOOPS" gave me the idea:

 

 

data:  ls_value_tab   type ty_table_line,
          lt_value_tab   type table of ty_table_line,
          ls_response    type ty_basetype,
          lt_response    type standard table of ty_basetype,
          lv_count       type i.

    field-symbols: <data>        type any,
                   <data2>       type any table,
                   <objects>     type any,
                   <object>      type any,
                   <properties>  type any,
                   <property>    type any,
                   <id>          type string,
                   <type>        type string,
                   <cardinality> type string,
                   <value_tab>   type any table.

assign lo_json_data->* to <data>.
        assign component 'OBJECTS' of structure <data> to <data2>.

        if sy-subrc = 0 and <data2> is assigned.

          loop at <data2> assigning <objects>.
            assign component 'OBJECT' of structure <objects> to <object>.

            if sy-subrc = 0 and <object> is assigned.
              assign component 'PROPERTIES' of structure <object> to <properties>.

              if sy-subrc = 0 and <properties> is assigned.
                do.
                  lv_count = lv_count + 1.
                  assign component lv_count of structure <properties> to <property>.
                  if sy-subrc <> 0 or  not <property> is assigned.
                    exit.
                  else.
                    assign component 'ID'          of structure <property> to <id>.
                    assign component 'TYPE'        of structure <property> to <type>.
                    assign component 'CARDINALITY' of structure <property> to <cardinality>.

                    if <cardinality> = 'single'.
                      assign component 'VALUE' of structure <property> to field-symbol(<value>).
                    else.
                      assign component 'VALUE' of structure <property> to <value_tab>.
                    endif.

                    ls_response-id          = <id>.
                    ls_response-type        = <type>.
                    ls_response-cardinality = <cardinality>.
                    if ls_response-cardinality = 'single'.
                      ls_value_tab-tline = <value>.
                      append ls_value_tab to lt_value_tab.
                      ls_response-value = lt_value_tab.
                    else.
                      loop at <value_tab> assigning <value>.
                        ls_value_tab-tline = <value>.
                         append ls_value_tab to lt_value_tab.
                      endloop.
                      ls_response-value = lt_value_tab.
                    endif.
                    clear: lt_value_tab[], ls_value_tab.
                    append ls_response to lt_response.
                  endif.
                enddo.
              endif.
            endif.
          endloop.
​

entry.title: Re: Variant configuration non material parameters more
entry.content:
Normally, there are two critical parts for VC.

1. Which according the Variant continuation choose.

That will be refer to the  constraints  setting, you should set the dependency for the different characteristic.

For example.

CT04 create the characteristic.

PC_Model (X1 X3)

PC_HARDDISK (250G 500G)

PC_MEMORY (4G 8G 16G)

Then create a class (TYPE=300) to combine the characteristics. （T-code:CL02）

And assign the Class to the material, and when you choose the PC_MODEL, the system can decide the Value of PC_MEMORY if you set the constrains.

2. Create a supper BOM, which contains all the possible materials list.

EX.

ITEM 0010： Material = 250GDISK 

Then set the Pre-condition in  OD for material 250GDISK ( PC_HARDDISK = '250G' ), so when you choose the PC_HARDDISK = '250G' in Sales order, this component item will be chosen.

ITEM 0020： Material = 500GDISK

Then set the Pre-condition in  OD for Material 500GDISK ( PC_HARDDISK = '500G' ), so when you choose the PC_HARDDISK = '500G' in Sales order, this component item will be chosen.

For the VC, there are so many information, that you can check the SAP Help to learn more.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/a73402f511734e6eac56063e631bf24e/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html

entry.title: SAP Presents AI Innovations for the Energy Sector at Enlit Europe in Milan
entry.content:
SAP will be attending Enlit Europe in Milan this year. The event brings together industry leaders, experts and innovators to discuss the latest trends and technologies in the energy sector.

Our utilities experts Mateu Munar and Thomas Mueller will be joining Enlit Europe and will be available for customer and partner discussions. They will be on hand to talk about SAP's latest solutions and innovations in the energy sector and how they can help companies achieve greater efficiency and innovation. Please reach out to them via the Enlit app. 

SAP will not only be available for talks but will also participate in the “AI Turbocharger Session”: AI boosting efficiency and accelerating innovation”, taking place in the Digitalisation Hub in Hall 5 on October 23 at 10:30am. During this session, SAP will have the opportunity to demonstrate how its AI solutions are helping to revolutionize the energy industry.

 

If you are attending Enlit Europe in Milan, be sure to get in touch with my colleagues and arrange a meeting with Mateu Munar and Thomas Mueller. With their expertise and SAP's innovative solutions, you will be well equipped.

Find more general information on the Enlit Europe website.

entry.title: Self-confident on stage: How I learned to manage my Inner Critic
entry.content:
Here I was, standing in front of my customer’s project team, of whom I didn’t know anyone besides the project manager. My task: inspire those 30 people for the upcoming software project. I have held presentations like this countless times. I could almost pray them down in sleep - and yet: every time: this nasty fluffy feeling in my stomach.

I often wondered where this feeling of insecurity came from. In my research, I came across many interesting theories, including the theme of the “Inner Critic”, “Inner Saboteur” or “Inner Script”. This theory describes, so to speak, an “inner voice” that makes us show a certain behavior or prevents us from doing certain things. Often the inner critic arises from past experiences, fears, or deeply rooted uncertainties. And of course it is not only linked to public speaking, but holistically to our lives and social interactions.

Inner critics can arise, for example, from beliefs. These are assumptions deeply anchored in our subconscious mind, which may come, for example, from our childhood or fundamentally from experiences with other people. Beliefs can certainly be helpful if, for example, they prescribe us to behave in a socially compliant way. For example, if you often hear “Be quiet! Hold back!” from your parents or teachers in your childhood, you might internalize: “I must not be loud.” This can be helpful in dosed use – for example in a learning environment at school. However, if you internalize this belief too strongly, it can mean that even in adulthood you generally (and unconsciously) withhold yourself or reluctantly express your opinion against others.

What makes things more difficult is that you do not only live with just one, but with a whole series of beliefs and internal critics – so the challenge can be multifaceted.

Luckily, the power of beliefs and the resulting internal critics can also be defused. In a first step, it is important to become aware of your beliefs and how your inner critic affects you.  Once identified, you can start to question what your saboteur asks of you and possibly set an end to self-fulfilling prophecies and vicious circles that hinder your performance. As a result, you transform negative beliefs into realistic self-images and thus act more freely and confidently – not only on stage.

Calming and managing your Inner Critic can be learned. One workshop has helped me a lot, which we now offer at SAP Women in Tech as a part of our SAP Women in Tech Speaker Journey. In this workshop, we create awareness of the related psychological mechanisms. The participants learn how to get on to their personal inner saboteur and acquire strategies to hold it at bay.  Of course, it takes willpower and repeated training to free oneself from practiced behavioral patterns, but it is indeed worth the hard work.

For my part, I learned over time to recognize when my personal saboteur sneaked up. With some practice, I can now deliberately silence him. For me personally, I figured that presenting more often and actively looking for opportunities to do so helped tremendously. Once the fluffy feeling in my stomach comes up, the positive feedback and affirmations of the audience encourage me, calm my inner critic, and make my presentations ever more confident.

What beliefs influence you when presenting? How do you deal with your inner saboteur? Do you have strategies on how to silence him (or at least make him “whisper”)? I am looking forward to your tips and inspirations!

entry.title: Re: 🟢 What Software Developers Should Know About Key User Extensibility
entry.content:
Hi Roger, 

you can expect that more and more Fiori apps are supporting the adaptation mode. Generally speaking all apps built with Fiori Elements do support the adaptation mode. For freestyle SAPUI5 apps, it depends on the controls. If they use so-called "smart controls" these controls also support the adaptation mode. So in these apps you will find sections that support the UI adaptation mode while other areas do not.

Thomas

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite Modernization: Percentage Breakdown of Project Phases
entry.content:
Introduction

Introduction

In a recent session on SAP Integration Suite modernization, we received a detailed percentage breakdown of project phases, including Technical Interface Migration, Testing, Partner Communication, and Cutover. While these estimates offer a helpful starting point, the specifics can vary greatly depending on various factors. This blog will explore the key aspects that can dramatically shift these percentages and affect the overall project outcome.

The Devil Lies in the Details

During one of the recent sessions on SAP Integration Suite modernization hosted by CBS (special thanks to Stefan Münchow), the team presented a breakdown of project phases, including Technical Interface Migration, Testing, Partner Communication, and Cutover (refer to Figure 1).

Figure 1 - Percentage Breakdown of Project Phases of SAP Integration Suite migration by CBS (Stefan Munchow)

However, this breakdown significantly differs from insights provided by Gartner in the article "How to Successfully Migrate to a New Integration Platform" by Abhishek Singh (October 1, 2021 but no longer available), which noted, "Testing is a substantial part of the migration effort, consuming around 60% to 65% of the overall work." How do we reconcile the discrepancy between estimates ranging from 25% to 60-65%? Which numbers are more accurate?

Figure 2 – 65% or 25% or maybe both?

It Depends...

My family often jokes about my frequent response, "It depends," and in this case, it rings true. Several factors can explain the large variance in these numbers, and I will break them down in detail.

Scope of the SAP Integration Suite Program

One key factor that can affect the percentage breakdown is whether we are accounting for the development team's efforts alone or including everyone involved in the project. For example, if a Service Integrator or Global Service Integrator presents a project breakdown, they may focus only on their responsibilities, excluding tasks like Functional Testing, User Acceptance Testing, or Partner Communication. These tasks typically fall under the company’s project management team.

Testing performed by developers who know the interfaces may only take 25% of the development effort, but if you account for Functional Testing and User Acceptance Testing—where users must test all scenarios—testing could take up 60-65% of the overall work. Similarly, Partner Communication efforts, especially when involving hundreds or thousands of EDI partners, could stretch out over months, not days.

Figure 3 - Ask questions on the scope  

Important Tip:
When reviewing a percentage breakdown of project phases, ask whether internal resources (functional consultants) and coordination with external B2B partners for communication tests are included.

Type of SAP Integration Suite Migration (Lift and Shift vs. Modernization)

With lift and shift (and all the automation related to that: Elevate from SAP Process Orchestration and Modernize your integration platform with SAP Integration Suite and Introducing the new pipeline concept in Cloud Integration) it’s hard to imagine the development effort would be 50%  (unless migrations would take a few days) but when you do a mix (lift and shift with a mix of new functions like use of AEM scenarios) then 50% of the development scope (not entire modernization as per the previously mentioned chapter) all of a sudden sounds reasonable. Some of my thoughts on this topic can also be found here: SAP BTP Integration Suite migration: don't do lift&shift!

Size of the SAP Integration Landscape (Number of Interfaces)

The size of your SAP integration landscape (e.g., 50-200 vs. 500-5000 interfaces) can greatly affect the percentage breakdown of project phases.

Figure 4 - Size is important 

Why should the percentage Breakdown of Project Phases of SAP Integration Suite matter because of the size?

The bigger you are the more important the project – that means that large (500-5000 interfaces migration projects) tend to be larger investments which means the company might be (does not mean it’s always the case) willing to invest more to make sure all goes well = potentially more skilled resources, more experience project management, more authority to push functional and business teams for testing. Is that always the case? Not at all but it might influence the percentage breakdown.

The more important your company the easier it is to communicate with partners – imagine doing an SAP Integration Suite migration being a one country company connected with 3-5 biggest retailers. Do you think they will easily allocate anytime easily? On the other hand imagine you’re a big retail company or one of the largest FMCG companies – do you think they will struggle as much with communication with their partners when they need to change something?

Important Tip:
When discussing the breakdown of project phases with a Service Integrator, be clear about your company's influence when negotiating with B2B partners.

Test Automation vs. Manual Testing

Testing manually for smaller projects (up to 50-100 interfaces) is feasible but inefficient. Automating the testing process can save significant time. Solutions like SAP's recommended automation tools - Automate over 65% of your SAP Integration Suite migration with Int4 solutions  and SAP services - Accelerate move to SAP Integration Suite with SAP Integration Suite Testing Service, can reduce testing time by over 65%, making a compelling case for automation in SAP Integration Suite migrations. They also support the latest automated migraton concepts - SAP Pipeline Concept and B2B TPM testing. 

Important Tip:
Consider automating the testing of SAP Integration Suite migrations. Learn more about testing automation through resources like the Int4 Shield Crash Course here.

Summary

The percentage breakdown of project phases in SAP Integration Suite modernisation projects can vary dramatically based on factors like the scope of the program, the type of migration, the size of the integration landscape, and whether testing is automated or manual. These factors can significantly alter the time and resources needed for phases such as Testing and Partner Communication, resulting in percentages ranging from 25% to 65%. Properly understanding and addressing these elements ensures a more accurate estimation of project efforts, reducing surprises and enabling smoother project execution.

entry.title: Learn how to get an S-user ID
entry.content:
SAP S-user ID, or S-user, is a unique identifier that allows you to login to your SAP products, report a case, download software, configure connections, and more. Your S-user ID contains the letter ‘S’ followed by a string of numbers (e.g. S00*******). 

You may come across the term ‘Super administrator’ and wonder how that differs from S-user. The super administrator within the SAP product your company is using (e.g. SAP Analytics Cloud) encompasses all administrative duties and has t highest level of authorization. The Super administrator is usually the person in your organization who originally purchased the SAP product. 

 

How are S-user IDs assigned? 

For new customers, the individual who purchased the SAP product will receive a welcome email containing their S-user ID. In this case, SAP assigns the first S-user ID. 

 

 The Super administrator can assign S-user IDs to various people in their organization, such as managers, who may need admin-level status. Everyone else in the company who uses the product does not necessarily need to have S-user IDs, they can simply login using their work email address once the Super administrator or S-user grants them access. 

 

 Please note, SAP cannot assign additional S-user IDs on your company’s behalf. 
 
If your organization has only one Super Administrator who has left, please contact the Customer Interaction Center to nominate a new Super Administrator. Ensure to provide the following information: 
 

 Ensure that the new nominee has below details: 

S-user ID (if already created): 

First name: 

Surname: 

Email address (note- domain of the email id should match company name): 

Phone number with country code: 

Designation: 

Reason for new super administrator nomination. 

 
To ensure that a new Super Admin can be nominated and granted Super Admin rights, please confirm that your organization has a valid maintenance agreement with SAP and that the installation is active. 

 

Let’s look at the steps, as how the company’s super admin creates an S-user ID. 

  1. Logon to SAP for Me and go to User Management  
  2. Click Request User on the top of the application 

     3. Fill in the Request User form 

  1. Click Submit 
  2. User was requested, click OK 
  • The user request is not instant and may take some time to complete. 
  • The new user will receive an e-mail from SAP Notification Services with the new S-user ID and an activation link, which is valid for 10 days. 
  • You can check the status of the new user on the Requested Users tab. Once the user is complete it will move to the Users tab. 
  • After the user is created, click the user ID and add a phone number or change any available details if needed. 
  • Assign authorizations to the user ID (see KBA 1511008). 

 

 

How to delete S-user IDs 

  1. In the User Management application, go to the Users tab 
  2. Scroll down the list or use the filter feature to find the S-user ID(s) to be deleted 
  3. Click the checkbox in front of the user(s) 
  4. Click Delete (if an error appears around important functions see KBA 1271517) 

 

 

  • It may take up to 24 hours to process your request for deletion. 
  • Deleted S-user IDs are listed in the Deleted Users tab of the application for twelve months and cannot be reactivated (KBA 1811859). 
  • Only a user that holds Super Administrator rights can delete another Super Administrator. 
  • S-user ID with important function (or critical roles) can be deleted only once those functions have been transferred to another S-user ID within the same company ERP number (KBA 1271517). 
  • Every customer account requires at least one Super Administrator. Therefore, you cannot delete all Super Administrators under your account. In exceptional cases when there is no active maintenance linked to your account, all Super Administrators can be deleted by SAP, provided you Account Executive confirms the request. 

For issues or questions on this topic, report a case for component XX-SER-FORME. Be sure to include details, S-user ID, screenshots, etc. to assist in troubleshooting. 

  • Online help for User, Authorization, and Administrator Concept 
  • KBA 2443869 - S-user ID authorization guide - SAP for Me 
  • KBA 1511008 - How to add or change S-user ID authorizations - SAP for Me 
  • KBA 1282854 - Information on adding/editing/deleting authorizations for S-user IDs - SAP for Me 
  • KBA 1614304 - How to use the Copy User's Authorization feature for S-user IDs - SAP for Me 

 

 

And now let’s check on how you can find the Super Admin details of your company 

  • Just go to me.sap.com and then >’Users &Contacts’ > My Important Contacts and you will see the list of all the S-users with special roles for each installation. 

 

If you want to make changes in the list of Important Contacts, please see these KBAs: 
 
How to maintain Super, Cloud, and User Administrator S-user IDs listed in My Important Contacts - SAP for Me 

How to change Software Recipient and Technical Contact users for on-premise installations listed in My Important Contacts - SAP for Me 

Thanks for reading!   
  
We would love to hear from you, let us know what you think? 😊 

 If you have any questions, please feel free to ask us in the Q&A All Questions in Customer Experience | SAP Community and All Questions in Support Services | SAP Community   

 

Read more #Customer Interaction Centre blogposts Here!

entry.title: Localized entity data not showing translated texts
entry.content:
I am developing a CAP application which has simple Employee - Department relationship.
 
Employee entity
entity Employees {
    key id        : String(15);
        firstname : String(25);
        lastname  : String(25);
        gender    : Association to Gender;
        dob: Date;
        email: String;
        phone: Phone;
        department: Association to Department;
}
 
Department entity
@Fiori.draft.enabled
entity Department: cuid {
    key name: String;
        description: localized String
}
 
The department entity can hold description in different languages.
Managing department entity
But when I try to display the Employee data along with the translated department description, it is not showing anything.
Annotation.cds
 
annotate service.Employees with @(
    UI.FieldGroup #GeneratedGroup : {
        $Type : 'UI.FieldGroupType',
        Data : [
// Some redacted code for Object Page
 
        ],
    },
    UI.Facets : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.ReferenceFacet',
            ID : 'GeneratedFacet1',
            Label : 'General Information',
            Target : '@UI.FieldGroup#GeneratedGroup',
        },
    ],
    UI.LineItem : [
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label : 'Partner Id',
            Value : id,
            ![@UI.Importance] : #High,
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label : 'First Name',
            Value : firstname,
            ![@UI.Importance] : #High,
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label : 'Last Name',
            Value : lastname,
            ![@UI.Importance] : #High,
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Label : 'Gender',
            Value : gender_gender,
            ![@UI.Importance] : #High,
        },
        {
            $Type : 'UI.DataField',
            Value : department.description,
            Label : 'Department',
        },
    ],
);
 
annotate service.Employees with {
    department @(
        Common.ValueList : {
        $Type : 'Common.ValueListType',
        CollectionPath : 'Department',
        Parameters : [
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty : department_name,
                ValueListProperty : 'name',
            },
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty : 'description',
            }
        ],
        Label : 'Department',
    },
        Common.ValueListWithFixedValues : true,
    )
};
 
annotate service.Employees with {
    organization @Common.ValueList : {
        $Type : 'Common.ValueListType',
        CollectionPath : 'Organization',
        Parameters : [
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty : organization_ID,
                ValueListProperty : 'ID',
            },
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty : 'name',
            },
        ],
    }
};
 
annotate service.Employees with {
    employment @(
        Common.ValueList : {
        $Type : 'Common.ValueListType',
        CollectionPath : 'EmploymentType',
        Parameters : [
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterInOut',
                LocalDataProperty : employment_name,
                ValueListProperty : 'name',
            },
            {
                $Type : 'Common.ValueListParameterDisplayOnly',
                ValueListProperty : 'description',
            },
        ],
        Label : 'Employment',
    },
        Common.ValueListWithFixedValues : true,
    )
};
 
annotate service.Department with {
    name @Common.Text : description
};
 
 
 
Service.cds
 
 
service AdminService {
    @odata.draft.enabled
    entity Employees as projection on schema.Employees;
    @odata.draft.enabled
    entity Department as projection on schema.Department;
}

entry.title: Re: PI 7.5 - INVALID_CHARACTER_ERR: An invalid or illegal...
entry.content:
Hi, 

I solved the error adding a "description name" to the colum. 

The scenario I had, I was checking the number of entries in a table with a simple query like

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM MY_TABLE

This is CPI error feedback: 

I managed to solve the problem just adding a column name to the COUNT(*) function. 

Like this: SELECT COUNT(*) AS "Count" FROM MY_TABLE. 

Seems like the error was in the "*" character when reading the payload's feedback.

entry.title: Transport from Q system to P system
entry.content:
We needs to perform transfer from Q system to P system. Will this influence our production system working right now. We have basic IC working right now in production. or can you advice how to proceed partial transfer? Or is it possible to reverse advanced IC and wait for this to function properly. 


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Transport from Q system to P system
entry.content:
Any changes that are made in the q system, will be captured in the transport request. So, while moving the respective transport, all the changes which has been made in the Q system will also be moved to the Production system.

There is no concept called partial transfer or reverse the transport request which has been imported in the production. If you do not want to move something, then revert the changes in Q,  and then again move it to Production.

You may also refer our SAP Help Portal documentation: Phases and Systems | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Re: Starting out developing skills in analytics? Not sure where to start?
entry.content:
Hi Rahul,

Firstly, well-done to you for developing skills in the two fastest-moving and current analytics solutions from SAP. SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere is a perfect pairing: the full suite extract, model and analyze. There are just so many analytic environments that it is just about impossible to suggest a single path for skills development. If you want to be in high demand with SAP customers, then I'd say BW/4HANA is a very good choice. There are more than 10,000 SAP customers who run BW/4HANA and need your help to transition from extract and modeling with BW/4HANA to SAP Datasphere. Especially learn about SAP Datasphere, BW Bridge to get them there:
Modeling in SAP Datasphere, SAP BW Bridge

Also, take a look at HANA calculation views modeling:
Working with Calculation Views in SAP HANA Cloud

and ETL/ELT with HANA data provisioning:
Provisioning Data to SAP HANA

Other non-SAP technologies such as R, and other coding languages are useful but they can consume a lot of your time to develop skills and you may find they are not always used in projects.

It would be interesting to hear what other learners think of these suggestions.

Hope this helps

Mark

Let me share my approach how to find learning material for the role Project manager

  1. On the SAP Learning homepage  use the search field and type in "project management"

entry.title: Manual migrate XSLT Mapping from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
In this blog, we check out how we can manually migrate XSLT mapping from SAP Process Orchestration to SAP Integration Suite.

The intention is to give an overview and entry point if you require, besides the well-known tools, manual intervention for a mapping.

Background: 

  • We have an original XSLT mapping on PO containing a lot of includes as well as java coding. Taking the original XSLT and ZIP from ESR won’t potentially work out of the box.

Approach

  • Bring the includes and path to the same level as the main XSLT mapping itself.
BeforeAfter

 

 

  • Check XSLT mapping and includes for Java functions and adjust the namespace as in the example below:
  • Change: new namespace based on the Java class name, exclude namespace from result, adjust function call with the namespace.
  • Important: simple Java functions should work. If you have complex Java functions like LookUp, please consider a redesign with Groovy, etc.
BeforeAfter
  • Create a new JAR file containing the Java class files according to the namespace path in the XSLT mapping.
  • Recommendation: Use NWDS/Eclipse for the setup of the JAR file
BeforeAfter

 

 

  • Upload the XSLT mapping and includes to "References" in your Integration flow (as a ZIP or via multi-selection in the upload dialog).

  • Upload the "JAR" File also to "References" in your Integration flow

 

Conclusion

  • As long as it is possible, consider using the migration tool for an automatic migration from PI/PO.
  • If a manual adjustment is required, this may give you an entry point for your investigation on XSLT mapping.
  • Keep in consideration: Migration is okay; however, modernization of your integration landscape and architecture also leads to better business outcomes

Further links:

Enable Migration Assessment Application 

Use the Migration Assessment Application 

Migration Guide for SAP Process Orchestration

entry.title: Re: Enabling In-Order processing with SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh Partitioned Queues
entry.content:
Hi @Hari-Rangarajan ,
While the blog focuses on partitioned queues, it seems to indicate that you can only achieve EOIO with PQs, which is not the complete picture.

Both PQs or Exclusive queues can be used for that purpose.
But I'm missing one other key aspect:
More important are other settings like the windowing, to only have one single message in-flight, to avoid the messages passing each other on the transport and handling retries correctly in the application layer (CPI in this case). We generally recommend that if one needs to maintain ordering across redeliveries, one should set the queue's max-delivered-unacked-msgs-per-flow to 1.

Why is this missing? Is this not necessary with PQs and CPI?

entry.title: Dynamically Hiding Table Columns in Print Forms with Adobe LiveCycle Designer and JavaScript
entry.content:
Print Form Blogs

This blog is part of a series dedicated to print form adaptation with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer. If you are interested in this topic, I recommend checking out my other blogs on the following list and my live session about print forms!

List of print form blogs:

  • How to get XML data for local preview of print forms without output history
  • Print Form Adaptation in Sales/Service 2: Get XML data for preview in Adobe LiveCycle Designer
  • Data Binding Basics in Print Forms
  • Data Binding with Repeating Items and Simple Tables in Print Forms
  • Data Binding with Repeating Items and Complex Tables in Print Forms
  • Building Tables with Scripts
  • Dynamically Hiding Table Columns in Print Forms with Adobe LiveCycle Designer and JavaScript (this blog)

 

Introduction

Most print forms that you work on with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer contain tables. While designing tables and binding data correctly can sometimes already be a challenge, I want to dedicate this blog to adapting tables during runtime, or to be more specific: How we can hide columns dynamically based on the data that they contain (or do not contain).

I wrote a whole blog post about that topic because it turned out that it's not as easy as one probably expects. The major part of this blog is the description of the journey, how I approached this task, what I discovered, and how I finally solved it. If you are only interested in the solution, you can directly jump to the respective section.

Before we deep dive into the topic, let's have a look at sample that I'll use to explain the backgrounds and demonstrate the solution.

 

The Sample

The sample consists of a very simple print form that has a table tblTable with a header row hdrHeader and a body row rowMain. The header row is enabled to be displayed on the initial page and on subsequent pages (Object palette -> Pagination). The body row is bound to a repeating data item "$.FormQuote.Item[*]" and is enabled to be repeated per data item (Object palette -> Binding; see the previous screenshot).

The data structure for this example has been kept as simple as possible. As we want to focus on items, it doesn't contain much more. The following screenshot shows the beginning of the XML sample file that I use. The actual number of items doesn't matter, as long as they are enough to achieve a page break, which also depends on the height of your body row. Preparing the sample data to force a page break is an important step in testing the print form, because tables can have a different "behavior" on subsequent pages as we will see in this blog.

 

The First Approach (Not Sufficient!)

During design time, the Designer allows you to insert and delete columns. But if you would like to hide a table column permanently (rather then removing it), you won't find an option. The first approach would probably be to select the column (which is possible by clicking slightly above the column while the cursor changes to a down pointing arrow) and set its presence to "Hidden (Exclude From Layout)". However, this is disabled for table controls as the following screenshot shows:

Interestingly, although the Designer doesn't allow you to do this during design time, you can still do it during runtime using a script - and that's actually what we want, isn't it? Before we look closer at this approach, let's discuss first, how tables are structured internally.

A table in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer's object model is a special aggregation of subforms and other controls such as text fields or draws ("labels") that form the rows and cells. There is no column object as such. However, we can access all the cells of a column, meaning the cell in the header row and all cells of the body rows (due to data binding, the rendered form has several body rows).

The following FormCalc script placed in the initialize or ready:form event of the tblTable object of the sample shown above, hides the header cell and all body cells of the Description column:

 

$.hdrHeader.hdrDescription.presence = "hidden"
$.rowMain[*].colDescription.presence = "hidden"

 

Keep in mind that rowMain is bound to a repeating data item and hence exists multiple times in the final form structure when it's rendered. Hence we need to address the cells of all these rows. FormCalc lets us easily address them by simply writing rowMain[*]. JavaScript doesn't allow that and we would need a for-loop instead.

I have seen this approach with hiding the column cells quite often. The reason for that might be the combination of the typical approach (setting something to "hidden") and the fact that it works... up to a certain extent!

This approach works only as long as the table doesn't cross a page break or at least doesn't repeat the header row in such a case. Another downside of this approach is: The width of the columns gets shifted. This might not be a problem when all columns have the same size. In the example, I intentionally used columns of different sizes to point out the problem. The following screenshot shows the result of this approach in the Preview. Compare the header of both pages with the previous screenshot in the Design view.

You can clearly see that the Description column has been "successfully" hidden, but the Quantity column now shows up with that size. Moreover, when looking at the second page in the Preview, we can see that the Description header cell is shown again, but has the Quantity body cells underneath it, and the Unit header cell stands out without body cells under it.

Now you could say: When the body rows get repeated and we must use [*] to access them, we may need to apply the same pattern to the header row as this gets repeated on subsequent pages as well. Resulting in something like this: $.hdrHeader[*].hdrDescription.presence = "hidden"

Unfortunately, this doesn't change anything and is maybe a reason, why the Designer doesn't allow you to set table cells to "Hidden". Hence, we need another solution.

Let's have a closer look at the issue with the changed column widths first and find a way how we can tackle that part of the issue. How can we influence the width of a column? Maybe this gives us further insights on how to work with columns? Spoiler alert: It does!

Changing a Column's Width

We'll first concentrate at the design-time and then at the scripting approach. When you want to change the width of a column in the Design view, you can select a single cell of the column and change the width from the Layout palette. This changes the width of the whole column.

Changing the column width via scripting, works differently and is the first time, we work column-oriented. This becomes more obvious when selecting the table object in the Hierarchy window and activating the XML Source view. (In case you don't see the XML Source tab yet, you can enable it from the application menu View -> XML Source. After enabling it from that menu, the tab will stay visible.)

In the XML Source view, you can see that tblTable is a special subform with the layout "table". The attribute columnWidths contains the width for each column as the previous screenshot shows. That means, in contrast to the design view, where you can influence the column width from a cell, the object model stores the width for each column "centrally" on the table level from where it can also be changed. Especially when working with complex tables, the Designer sometimes doesn't allow you to change the column width by dragging and dropping the column or even from the Layou palette. In such cases, you can usually change the column width from the XML Source view.

Caution: If you make a mistake while editing the XML and the Designer can no longer load it, the template is destroyed (at least as long as you can't fix it on your own). For that reason, I suggest to create a backup of the xdp-file before working in the XML Source.

Also, pay attention to the amount of values that the columnWidths attribute contains. In some print forms, I found more values than the table had columns, which can lead to the problem that the Designer doesn't allow you to edit the widths via the Layout palette or when it allows you to do so, some unexpected things may happen.

Coming back to the scriping approach: When you want to change the width of a column via scripting, you can access the same attribute via the object model. The following line of code changes the width of the second column from 40mm to 30mm:

tblTable.columnWidths = "15mm 30mm 100mm 18mm 30mm";

Hiding Columns

With the columnWidths attribute, we can not only change the width of columns, it is finally also the way to hide them! Have you tried to set the value for a single column to 0mm ? Sounds promising, but this only puts the column into an auto-size mode. However, the correct value is very close: 0.01mm 😁

With 0.01mm, the column becomes "hidden". You can also assign this value to a cell via the Layout palette and the column will disappear. So, this is exactly what we want and we can even apply this with a script! Is this the solution we were looking for? Not entirely yet... There is another problem with this approach: If the hidden column contains body cells that are allowed to automatically grow (e.g. because they also allow line breaks within their content), you will face the following result:

What happens here? The sample data intentionally includes a multi line Description and hence the sample form is designed to deal with that. The Description cell has the following two options enabled:

  • Object palette -> Cell -> ✔️Allow Multiple Lines
  • Layout palette -> Height -> ✔️Expand to fit

Setting the Description column's size to 0.01mm doesn't mean, it's completely excluded from the layout as the presence "Hidden" would do. Instead, the Designer tries to display the Description with approximately one letter per line, because there isn't enough space with a width of 0.01mm, although the column is not really visible. This leads to these grown row heights.

But of course, there is also a solution for this problem: We must remove the content from "hidden" cell's by setting their rawValue to "".

To summarize that: In order to dynamically hide a column, we must change the columnWidths attribute, which requires some string operations for splitting and replacing the measurement values and we need to remove the content of all affected cells. That sounds like a task where a script snippet would be very welcome...

 

The Solution - Script: HideTableColumn

The JavaScript snippet below can be used to hide table columns dynamically. Unfortunately, it looks a bit crowded in the source block below due to forced line breaks. The parameters of the function are explained as part of the script comments and I'll explain how to use the script based on the sample below. As I had an additional requirement to only hide a certain table column, if all body cells were empty, the script also covers this case.

 

//Hides columns of a table. Place it in the ready:form event of the table, e.g.: HideTableColumn(this, 1, "rowMain[*].colProduct");
// !! Make sure that the header cells of the columns that are to be hidden are not allowed to expand their height! To ensure that, go to XML view and ensure that the field has "h" instead of "minH" !!
// oTable: Table object where the column belongs to -> simply "this" when the script call is placed in the table object itself
// columnIndex: Zero based index of the table column that is to be hidden
// somBodyCells: String containing the SOM Expression to the body cells (containing [*]) of the column relative to the table object, e.g. "rowMain[*].colDescription"
// onlyHideIfAllEmpty: bool -> if true, column will only be hidden if all cells are empty, otherwise column will be hidden in all cases
// returns true if the column has been hidden
function HideTableColumn(oTable, columnIndex, somBodyCells, onlyHideIfAllEmpty)
{
	var oBodyNodes = oTable.resolveNodes(somBodyCells);
	
	if (onlyHideIfAllEmpty)
	{
		for(var i = 0; i < oBodyNodes.length; i++)
		{
			var oField = oBodyNodes.item(i);
		 	// on the first occurence of any content, leave the function
			if (oField.rawValue > "")
				return false;
		}
	}
	
	for(var i = 0; i < oBodyNodes.length; i++)
	{
		// set the value to ""; setting the presence to "hidden" would scramble columns; "invisible" would introduce line breaks and expand heights
		oBodyNodes.item(i).rawValue = "";
	}	

	// setting columnWidth is necessary to hide the column of all subsequent pages
	var aWidths = oTable.columnWidths.split(" ");
	var newWidths = "";
	
	for (var i = 0; i < aWidths.length; i++)
	{
		if (i == columnIndex)
			newWidths += "0.01mm "; // hide a column
		else
			newWidths += aWidths[i] + " ";
	}
	
	oTable.columnWidths = newWidths;
		
	return true;
}

 

The script should be added to a scripting object to make it possible to call it from different places across the print form. Even if you need it only in a single place and could put it into the event where you also call it, my suggestion is to use a script object for "reuse" functions.

To add a script object, right click on the root element in the Hierarchy window and select Insert Script Object as the following screenshot shows. You can give the script object a name, for instance "utils".

Once the script snippet has been pasted in the script object, you can take care of calling the script. My suggestion is to do this in the ready:form event of the table where you want to hide the columns. In the sample case, we want to hide the Description column of the table tblTable, hence the calling script would look as follows (Make sure to choose JavaScript as the scripting language!):

 

// FormQuoteNotification.bdyMain.frmTable.tblTable::ready:form - (JavaScript, server)

utils.HideTableColumn(this, 2, "rowMain[*].colDescription", false);

 

When calling scripts that are part of a script object, you must include the script object's name. In my case, I have named the script object "utils", hence I have to call the function with utils.HideTableColumn(...);.

The parameters explained:

  • The first parameter oTable requires the table object. When you place the script in an event of the table, you can simply write this.
  • The second parameter columnIndex requires the zero-based column index. This will be used to split the columnWidths string and replace the respective part with 0.01mm. Hiding the third column means index 2.
  • The third parameter somBodyCells requires a string containing a so called SOM expression, which is the path to the body cell in the object model relative to the table, including the [*] notation, because we will have several body rows after rendering.
  • The last parameter onlyHideIfAllEmpty requires a boolean value (true/false) and specifies if the column should only be hidden, if all body cells are empty. That means "false" hides the column in all cases. "true" makes the script check if any cell contains text and if so, the column will be left visible.

Remark on the used event: Calling the script during the ready:form event, was the best solution in my tests. It also works when you call it in the initialize event of the table. In this case, there's only one special behavior that you should keep in mind: The script sets the rawValue of the affected body cells to "". When you do this during the initialize event, this is reflected in the data source! That means, if you have another table where you want to display the values of this column, this won't show values either. This behavior does not happen when you place the script in the ready:form event.

 

Summary

Dynamically hiding a table column in a print form with the Adobe LiveCycle Designer isn't as straight forward as one may wish. When I was looking for ways to hide columns, I was hoping for something like table.column[x].hidden = true;, but that doesn't exist.

The script of this blog post is my way to accomplish this task and has worked so far with the requirements I had. But I wouldn't be surprised if there's a much easier and better solution and I just didn't find it. If you know a better alternative, kindly let me know! 😉For everyone else, feel free to use the script and drop me some feedback! I hope it helps! 🙂

 

Want To Learn More? -> Live Sessions

I deliver monthly live sessions with a changing agenda on the topic of print form adaptation in different SAP solutions (currently SAP Sales and Service Cloud (the former SAP Cloud for Customer), SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, SAP Business ByDesign and SAP S/4HANA Cloud). The main part of the session is usually solution independent, as I work in the Adobe LiveCycle Designer, but I also mention solution specifics in every session. If you are interested and would like to join, here are the details:

Starting 2024 the live sessions have moved to a new platform: You can find them via this new direct link on learning.sap.com. You still need a corresponding subscription in order to register for the live session.

If you want to get informed about the next blogs of this series or updates on the live session, please follow this blog post by clicking on the green “follow” button on the left. You can also follow me fwys for more blog posts about SAP Customer Experience Solutions – Integration and Extensibility topics.

entry.title: Extract TPART Business Partner Functions (Texts) Data in CSV File Using ABAP Program
entry.content:
ABAP Program -  Z_TPART_CUSTOMER          

  •  Program Type – ABAP 
  • ABAP Program Name – Z_TPART_CUSTOMER   
  • Tcode - Z_TPART_CUST    
  • Entity Description - Business Partner Functions (Texts) Data 
  • Output File Location - “C:\tmp” 
  • Output File Name- TPART.CSV  
  • Output File Type – CSV (Column with Semicolon separator (;) 
  • SAP Table Name – TPART, KNVP, KNB1 
  • List of fields to be extracted -  
  • KUNNR 
  • VTEXT 

------ABAP PROGRAM START FROM HERE------------

REPORT Z_TPART_CUSTOMER.

TYPE-POOLS: VRM.

DATA: NAME TYPE VRM_ID,
LIST TYPE VRM_VALUES,
VALUE LIKE LINE OF LIST.


PARAMETERS: ENTITY(10) AS LISTBOX VISIBLE LENGTH 10.
PARAMETERS : OPATH TYPE STRING DEFAULT '\\SERVERNAME\tmp\TPART.csv'.

AT SELECTION-SCREEN OUTPUT.

NAME = 'ENTITY'.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK01'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK01'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK75'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK75'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK21'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK21'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK31'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK31'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK41'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK41'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK02'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK02'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK73'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK73'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK04'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK04'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

VALUE-KEY = 'UK61'.
VALUE-TEXT = 'UK61'.
APPEND VALUE TO LIST.

CALL FUNCTION 'VRM_SET_VALUES' EXPORTING ID = NAME VALUES = LIST.

START-OF-SELECTION.

type-pools:TRUXS.
data: begin of itab occurs 0,
KUNNR like KNVP-KUNNR,
VTEXT like TPART-VTEXT,
BUKRS like KNB1-BUKRS,
end of itab.

data: itab1 type TRUXS_T_TEXT_DATA.
SELECT DISTINCT KNVP~KUNNR
TPART~VTEXT
KNB1~BUKRS
FROM KNVP INNER JOIN KNB1
ON KNVP~KUNNR = KNB1~KUNNR INNER JOIN TPART ON TPART~PARVW = KNVP~PARVW
into table itab
WHERE KNB1~BUKRS = ENTITY.

CALL FUNCTION 'SAP_CONVERT_TO_CSV_FORMAT'
EXPORTING
I_FIELD_SEPERATOR = ','
TABLES
I_TAB_SAP_DATA = itab
CHANGING
I_TAB_CONVERTED_DATA = itab1
EXCEPTIONS
CONVERSION_FAILED = 1
OTHERS = 2
.
IF SY-SUBRC <> 0.
MESSAGE ID SY-MSGID TYPE SY-MSGTY NUMBER SY-MSGNO
WITH SY-MSGV1 SY-MSGV2 SY-MSGV3 SY-MSGV4.
ENDIF.

CALL FUNCTION 'GUI_DOWNLOAD'
EXPORTING
filename = OPATH
TABLES
data_tab = itab1
EXCEPTIONS
OTHERS = 1.

entry.title: Dynamically change the input type of a Parameters Variable.
entry.content:
Due to a particular requirement if a Basis layer decides to switch to a new table(Example: swu_wlscan2) instead of an old table(Example: swu_wlscan ) and thereby changing the value range of some of its Parameters(Example: Job Suffix), the application layer functionalities that use this table needs to adapt. The challenge here is that in application layer you are not sure that in a particular environment which basis release and support package is applicable and which of the 2 tables is active. In this case, if you have a program(Example/SAPSRM/OFFLINEAPPROVALSEND) in which there is Parameters declared for selection-screen and this Parameters type is that of the changed parameter in the Basis release, then there is problem statement of how to dynamically have this parameter type determined depending on the availability of the old or new table in Basis release. So that the application layer does not have to create a new program altogether to cater to the table changes.

You can solve the issue by

  1.     First replacing the static type declaration of Parameters to dynamic

Example:

     PARAMETERS: p_jobsuf LIKE SWU_WLSCAN-CURR_NUMB.

      Replace above with below statement

      PARAMETERS: p_jobsuf LIKE (lv_type). 

2.     Find the type of Dynamic type (Example:lv_type) in Initialization event block by checking the existence of the table.

INITIALIZATION.

CALL FUNCTION 'DD_EXIST_TABLE'
EXPORTING
  tabname      = 'SWU_WLSCAN2'
  status       = 'A'
IMPORTING
  subrc        = lv_subrc
EXCEPTIONS
  wrong_status = 1
  OTHERS       = 2.
IF lv_subrc <> 0.
  lv_type = 'SWU_WLSCAN-CURR_NUMB'.
ELSE.
  lv_type = 'SWU_WLSCAN2-CURR_NUMB'.
ENDIF.
